summary: Gruber examines why Apple devices prevent users from taking screenshots of DRM-protected streaming video, a restriction that doesn't exist on Windows. He explains that the blackout operates at the hardware level through deep OS-hardware integration, making it impossible to circumvent on iOS and difficult on macOS (though disabling Chrome's hardware acceleration works as a workaround). He argues this 'feature' is pointless since nobody would pirate video one frame at a time, and it hasn't stopped actual bootlegging. The restriction only frustrates honest users who want to capture stills for legitimate purposes like criticism, reference, or sharing.
key_points: DRM screenshot blocking on Apple devices operates at the hardware/GPU level, making it nearly impossible to circumvent, especially on iOS where there's no workaround
Windows allows screenshots of DRM video not because streaming services don't care, but because Windows has a less integrated imaging pipeline between OS and hardware
Disabling hardware acceleration in Chrome on Mac is a workaround that re-enables DRM video screenshots, proving the restriction is tied to GPU-level enforcement
The restriction serves no practical anti-piracy purpose since no one would bootleg video one screenshot at a time, and actual video piracy continues unimpeded
The feature disproportionately punishes legitimate users who want high-quality stills for criticism, research, or personal reference
money_quotes: No one is going to create bootleg copies of DRM-protected video one screenshotted still frame at a time — and even if they tried, they'd be capturing only the images, not the sound.

This 'feature' accomplishes nothing of value for anyone, including the streaming services, but imposes a massive (and for most people, confusing and frustrating) hindrance on honest people simply trying to easily capture high-quality screenshots of the shows and movies they're watching.
These DRM blackouts on Apple devices are enabled through the deep integration between the OS and the hardware, thus enabling the blackouts to be imposed at the hardware level.
It's not like this 'feature' in MacOS and iOS has put an end to bootlegging DRM-protected video content.
She was greeted by a black box where her flowers used to be, a censored version of what she had meant to capture.
You wouldn't want to browse like this all the time (certainly not on battery power), but it's a great trick to know for capturing stills from videos.
key_insight: Apple's hardware-level DRM screenshot blocking is a pointless restriction that does nothing to prevent actual piracy while punishing legitimate users trying to capture stills from streaming video.
